infobox.key: ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating
infobox.value: – originally known as the **Champions Series** – is a series of senior-level international [figure skating competitions](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union"). Though none of the events were new, they debuted as a unified series in 1995. The Grand Prix consists of six qualifying events, held in succession over a span of six weeks, plus the [Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final"). Currently, Grand Prix events are hosted by Canada ([Skate Canada International](/wiki/Skate_Canada_International "Skate Canada International")), China ([Cup of China](/wiki/Cup_of_China "Cup of China")), Finland ([Grand Prix of Finland](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Finland "Grand Prix of Finland")), France ([Grand Prix de France](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_France_(figure_skating) "Grand Prix de France (figure skating)")), Japan ([NHK Trophy](/wiki/NHK_Trophy "NHK Trophy")), and the United States ([Skate America](/wiki/Skate_America "Skate America")). Previous events were hosted by Germany ([Bofrost Cup on Ice](/wiki/Bofrost_Cup_on_Ice "Bofrost Cup on Ice")) and Russia ([Rostelecom Cup](/wiki/Rostelecom_Cup "Rostelecom Cup")). Medals are awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). Skaters earn points based on their results at their respective competitions, and after the six qualifying events, the top skaters or teams in each discipline are invited to compete at the Grand Prix Final.

* [Grand Prix de France](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_France_(figure_skating) "Grand Prix de France (figure skating)"): The first iteration of the Grand Prix de France – then called the Grand Prix International de Paris – was held in 1987 in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[16]](#cite_note-France1987-16) When the ISU launched the Champions Series in 1995, the Grand Prix de France was one of the five qualifying events.[[3]](#cite_note-SkatingAugust1995-3) It has been a Grand Prix event every year since, except for 2020, when it was cancelled due to the COVID-19 pandemic.*[[17]](#cite_note-COVID192-17)* The Grand Prix de France has been held under several names, including the Trophée de France,[[18]](#cite_note-France1995-18) the Trophée Lalique,[[19]](#cite_note-19) the Trophée Éric Bompard,[[20]](#cite_note-Bompard-20) and the Internationaux de France.[[21]](#cite_note-France2017-21)

The **ISU Grand Prix of Figure Skating** – originally known as the **Champions Series** – is a series of senior-level international [figure skating competitions](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") sanctioned by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union"). Though none of the events were new, they debuted as a unified series in 1995. The Grand Prix consists of six qualifying events, held in succession over a span of six weeks, plus the [Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final"). Currently, Grand Prix events are hosted by Canada ([Skate Canada International](/wiki/Skate_Canada_International "Skate Canada International")), China ([Cup of China](/wiki/Cup_of_China "Cup of China")), Finland ([Grand Prix of Finland](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Finland "Grand Prix of Finland")), France ([Grand Prix de France](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_France_(figure_skating) "Grand Prix de France (figure skating)")), Japan ([NHK Trophy](/wiki/NHK_Trophy "NHK Trophy")), and the United States ([Skate America](/wiki/Skate_America "Skate America")). Previous events were hosted by Germany ([Bofrost Cup on Ice](/wiki/Bofrost_Cup_on_Ice "Bofrost Cup on Ice")) and Russia ([Rostelecom Cup](/wiki/Rostelecom_Cup "Rostelecom Cup")). Medals are awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). Skaters earn points based on their results at their respective competitions, and after the six qualifying events, the top skaters or teams in each discipline are invited to compete at the Grand Prix Final.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISU_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Beginning with the 1995–96 season, the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU) launched the Champions Series – later renamed the Grand Prix Series – which consisted of five qualifying competitions and the Champions Series Final.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This allowed skaters to perfect their programs earlier in the season, as well as compete against the skaters whom they would later encounter at the [World Championships](/wiki/World_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Figure Skating Championships"). This series also provided the viewing public with additional televised skating, which had been in demand.[[2]](#cite_note-Hines20062-2) The five qualifying competitions during the inaugural season were the [1995 Nations Cup](/wiki/1995_Nations_Cup "1995 Nations Cup"), the [1995 NHK Trophy](/wiki/1995_NHK_Trophy "1995 NHK Trophy"), the [1995 Skate America](/wiki/1995_Skate_America "1995 Skate America"), the [1995 Skate Canada](/wiki/1995_Skate_Canada_International "1995 Skate Canada International"), and the [1995 Trophée de France](/wiki/1995_Troph%C3%A9e_de_France "1995 Trophée de France").[[3]](#cite_note-SkatingAugust1995-3) Skaters earned points based on their results in their respective competitions and the top skaters or teams in each discipline were invited to compete at the [Champions Series Final](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Champions_Series_Final "1995–96 Champions Series Final").[[2]](#cite_note-Hines20062-2)

The Grand Prix Series currently comprises the following competitions:

* [Skate Canada International](/wiki/Skate_Canada_International "Skate Canada International"): The first Skate Canada competition was held in 1973 in [Calgary, Alberta](/wiki/Calgary,_Alberta "Calgary, Alberta").[[4]](#cite_note-SkatingJanuary19732-4) When the ISU launched the Champions Series in 1995, Skate Canada International was one of the five qualifying events.[[3]](#cite_note-SkatingAugust1995-3) It has been a Grand Prix event every year since, except for 2020, when it was cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports#Figure_skating "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports").[[5]](#cite_note-COVID19-5)
* [Cup of China](/wiki/Cup_of_China "Cup of China"): The first Cup of China was held in 2003 in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") as a replacement for the [Bofrost Cup on Ice](/wiki/Bofrost_Cup_on_Ice "Bofrost Cup on Ice").[[6]](#cite_note-GoldenSkate-6) The Cup of China has been interrupted three times in its history: in 2018 when the [Chinese Skating Association](/wiki/Chinese_Skating_Association "Chinese Skating Association") elected to forgo hosting any international skating events in order to prepare its venues for the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics"),[[7]](#cite_note-No2018-7) and then in 2021 and 2022 due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[8]](#cite_note-COC2021-8)[[9]](#cite_note-COC2022-9)
* [Grand Prix of Finland](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Finland "Grand Prix of Finland"): The first Grand Prix of Finland was held in 2018 in [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki") as a replacement for the Cup of China.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It returned in 2022 as a replacement for the [Rostelecom Cup](/wiki/Rostelecom_Cup "Rostelecom Cup") after Russia was banned from hosting any international figure skating competitions following the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[11]](#cite_note-Rostelecom_Cup_Banned-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) This event has been held under several names, including the Grand Prix of Helsinki,[[13]](#cite_note-Finland2018-13) the Grand Prix of Espoo,[[14]](#cite_note-Finland2022-14) and the Finlandia Trophy**.[[15]](#cite_note-Finland2024-15)**
* [Grand Prix de France](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_France_(figure_skating) "Grand Prix de France (figure skating)"): The first iteration of the Grand Prix de France – then called the Grand Prix International de Paris – was held in 1987 in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[16]](#cite_note-France1987-16) When the ISU launched the Champions Series in 1995, the Grand Prix de France was one of the five qualifying events.[[3]](#cite_note-SkatingAugust1995-3) It has been a Grand Prix event every year since, except for 2020, when it was cancelled due to the COVID-19 pandemic.*[[17]](#cite_note-COVID192-17)* The Grand Prix de France has been held under several names, including the Trophée de France,[[18]](#cite_note-France1995-18) the Trophée Lalique,[[19]](#cite_note-19) the Trophée Éric Bompard,[[20]](#cite_note-Bompard-20) and the Internationaux de France.[[21]](#cite_note-France2017-21)
* [NHK Trophy](/wiki/NHK_Trophy "NHK Trophy"): The first NHK Trophy was held in 1979 in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo").[[22]](#cite_note-NHK1979-22) When the ISU launched the Champions Series in 1995, the NHK Trophy was one of the five qualifying events.[[3]](#cite_note-SkatingAugust1995-3) It has been a Grand Prix event every year since.
* [Skate America](/wiki/Skate_America "Skate America"): The first Skate America – then called the Norton Skate – was held in 1979 in [Lake Placid, New York](/wiki/Lake_Placid,_New_York "Lake Placid, New York"), as a test event for the [1980 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1980 Winter Olympics").[[23]](#cite_note-SA1979-23) When the ISU launched the Champions Series in 1995, Skate America was one of the five qualifying events.[[3]](#cite_note-SkatingAugust1995-3) It has been a Grand Prix event every year since.
* [Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final"): This event is the culminating event of the Grand Prix Series. Skaters earn points based on their results at qualifying competitions each season, and the top skaters or teams in each discipline are invited to then compete at the Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the [2020 Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2020–21 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") was at first postponed,[[24]](#cite_note-ISU-200930-24) and then cancelled in December 2020.*[[25]](#cite_note-2020Cancellation-25)* In response to the discovery of the [SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant](/wiki/Omicron_variant "Omicron variant") in November 2021, the [2021 Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2021–22 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") was cancelled as well.*[[26]](#cite_note-2021Cancellation-26)*

The following competitions have been discontinued:

* [Bofrost Cup on Ice](/wiki/Bofrost_Cup_on_Ice "Bofrost Cup on Ice"): The first iteration of the Bofrost Cup on Ice – then called the Fujifilm Trophy – was held in 1986 in [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt").[[27]](#cite_note-SkateCanada-27) When the ISU launched the Champions Series in 1995, the German competition – at that point called the Nations Cup – was one of the five qualifying events.[[3]](#cite_note-SkatingAugust1995-3) It remained a Grand Prix event until 2002, after which point it was supplanted by the Cup of China.[[6]](#cite_note-GoldenSkate-6) This event was held under several names, including the Sparkassen Cup on Ice.[[28]](#cite_note-Germany1998-28) Despite losing its spot in the Grand Prix series, the [German Ice Skating Union](/wiki/German_Ice_Skating_Union "German Ice Skating Union") continued to stage the competition, but it did not continue after 2004.[[29]](#cite_note-Germany2004-29)
* [Rostelecom Cup](/wiki/Rostelecom_Cup "Rostelecom Cup"): The first iteration of the Rostelecom Cup – originally known as the Cup of Russia – was held in 1996 in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg") as part of the Champions Series.[[30]](#cite_note-COR1996-30) It was a Grand Prix event every year until the ISU barred Russia from hosting international skating competitions after the Russian invasion of Ukraine in February 2022.[[11]](#cite_note-Rostelecom_Cup_Banned-11)

The following competitions were held only one time:

* [Gran Premio d'Italia](/wiki/2021_Gran_Premio_d%27Italia "2021 Gran Premio d'Italia"): The 2021 Cup of China was cancelled due to travel and quarantine restrictions related to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_China "COVID-19 pandemic in China").[[8]](#cite_note-COC2021-8) The Gran Premio d'Italia was held as a replacement event in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") in November 2021.[[31]](#cite_note-ISU-08272021-31)
* [MK John Wilson Trophy](/wiki/2022_MK_John_Wilson_Trophy "2022 MK John Wilson Trophy"): In July 2022, the ISU announced the cancellation of the 2022 Cup of China due to China's ongoing travel restrictions related to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[9]](#cite_note-COC2022-9) The MK John Wilson Trophy was held as a replacement event in [Sheffield, England](/wiki/Sheffield,_England "Sheffield, England"), in November 2022.[[32]](#cite_note-BIS07242022-32)

The International Skating Union established the [Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating "ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating") in 1997 as a comparable series to the Grand Prix series. It consists of a series of seven international figure skating competitions exclusively for junior-level skaters. The locations of the Junior Grand Prix events change every year. Skaters earn points based on their results each season, and the top skaters or teams in each discipline are then invited to compete at the [Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final").[[33]](#cite_note-:022-33) Beginning with the [2008–09 figure skating season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_figure_skating_season "2008–09 figure skating season"), the Grand Prix Final and Junior Grand Prix Final have been held concurrently.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
